title: F5 BIG-IP APM Pre-Auth RCE CVE-2025-53521 (CVSS 9.8) Added to CISA KEV with 3-Day Deadline: Nation-State Supply Chain Origins Enable Root-Level Compromise — BRICKSTORM Backdoor Deployed in Active Exploitation Campaign

timeline.event: F5 discovers nation-state (China-nexus UNC5221) intrusion in corporate networks; attackers access BIG-IP source code containing undisclosed vulnerabilities
F5 publicly discloses data breach; CVE-2025-53521 initially classified as a Denial-of-Service vulnerability; patches released (BIG-IP 17.1.0.4+, 16.1.4.3+, 15.1.10.2+)
CISA issues emergency directive requiring federal agencies to patch by this date (original DoS classification)
CISA publishes BRICKSTORM malware analysis report (AR25-338a) linking Go-based ELF backdoor to exploitation of F5 BIG-IP systems
Vulnerability reclassified from Denial-of-Service to Remote Code Execution following evidence of active exploitation with root-level access
CISA adds CVE-2025-53521 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog; federal agencies given urgent 3-day remediation deadline
Remediation deadline for all U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies

simple_summary.what_happened: A critical vulnerability in F5 BIG-IP Access Policy Manager (APM) — a widely used network security appliance deployed at Fortune 500 companies and government agencies — allows attackers to take complete control of the device without needing a username or password. The flaw originated from a nation-state hack of F5's own development systems in August 2025, where attackers stole source code and discovered this hidden vulnerability before F5 did. The vulnerability was initially misclassified as a less serious 'denial of service' bug, causing many organizations to delay patching. It was reclassified as full remote code execution in March 2026 when active exploitation was confirmed. CISA added it to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on March 27, 2026, with an unprecedented 3-day deadline for federal agencies.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running F5 BIG-IP APM versions 15.1.0-15.1.10, 16.1.0-16.1.6, 17.1.0-17.1.2, and 17.5.0-17.5.1 with an access policy configured on a virtual server. This includes U.S. federal agencies, Fortune 500 companies, financial institutions, healthcare organizations, and critical infrastructure operators worldwide. F5 BIG-IP APM is particularly prevalent in enterprise and government environments as a network access and security gateway.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attackers scan the internet for exposed F5 BIG-IP APM management interfaces and virtual servers with access policies enabled, often targeting the REST API endpoint at /mgmt/shared/identified-devices/config/device-info. Step 2: A specially crafted HTTP/HTTPS request is sent directly to the BIG-IP APM virtual server — no credentials or authentication are required for this step. Step 3: The malicious request triggers a memory corruption flaw in the apmd (Access Policy Manager daemon) process, which handles live traffic, allowing injection of arbitrary code. Step 4: Attackers gain root-level command execution on the underlying operating system of the BIG-IP appliance, effectively owning the network gateway. Step 5: Attackers deploy BRICKSTORM — a Go-based ELF backdoor — which uses nested TLS encryption and DNS-over-HTTPS to hide its command-and-control traffic from security monitoring tools. Step 6: Using their persistent foothold on the BIG-IP gateway, attackers conduct network reconnaissance, steal credentials, exfiltrate sensitive data, and pivot laterally into the internal corporate or government network behind the appliance.
what_you_should_do: Immediately patch all affected F5 BIG-IP APM systems to fixed versions: 17.1.0.4 or later, 16.1.4.3 or later, or 15.1.10.2 or later
If immediate patching is not possible, restrict network access to BIG-IP management interfaces and REST API endpoints (port 443/8443) to trusted IP ranges only
Run F5's system integrity verification tools to check for file modifications — particularly look for changes to /usr/bin/umount and /usr/sbin/httpd, and unexpected files in /etc/sysconfig/
Hunt for BRICKSTORM indicators: suspicious /run/bigtlog.pipe files, unusual DNS-over-HTTPS queries from BIG-IP systems, and HTTP 201 responses with CSS content-type headers
Review BIG-IP audit logs for unexpected iControl REST API calls from localhost processes and any apmd process crashes or restarts
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This vulnerability is exceptionally dangerous for three reasons. First, the supply chain origin means attackers had advance knowledge of the vulnerability before defenders did — China-nexus threat group UNC5221 had over 12 months of access to F5's source code, allowing them to develop exploits before any patches existed. Second, the initial misclassification as 'denial of service' caused many organizations to deprioritize patching, meaning a large portion of the affected install base remains vulnerable. Third, F5 BIG-IP APM sits at the perimeter of enterprise networks as a security gateway — compromising it gives attackers access to everything behind it, including internal networks, credentials, and sensitive data. The 3-day CISA deadline signals the U.S. government believes exploitation is widespread and immediate.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit CVE-2025-53521 in F5 BIG-IP APM virtual servers exposed to the internet, gaining pre-authentication remote code execution without any credentials
After exploitation, attackers execute arbitrary OS commands as root on the BIG-IP appliance using command-line interfaces
BRICKSTORM and related implants modify /etc/sysconfig/ and boot initialization scripts to survive system reboots

tactics.technique_name: Hijack Execution Flow: Path Interception by PATH Environment Variable
tactics.description: Attackers hijack PATH environment to ensure malicious binaries execute instead of legitimate system tools, evading detection
BRICKSTORM backdoor uses nested TLS encryption over HTTPS and abuses DNS-over-HTTPS for C2 communications, making traffic appear as normal web traffic
From the compromised BIG-IP gateway, attackers use SSH and proxying capabilities to move laterally into the internal network behind the appliance
Stolen credentials and sensitive data are exfiltrated through encrypted BRICKSTORM C2 channels disguised as normal web traffic

detection_guidance.summary: Detection requires a combination of file integrity monitoring on the BIG-IP appliance, network traffic analysis for abnormal outbound connections, and log review for suspicious REST API activity. Organizations should prioritize hunting for BRICKSTORM indicators given the backdoor's stealth characteristics.

log_sources.description: Contains records of REST API calls, configuration changes, and process activity on the BIG-IP appliance; look for unexpected iControl REST API calls originating from localhost processes
Records apmd process activity, virtual server traffic events, and access policy decisions; look for apmd crashes or restarts which may indicate exploitation attempts
Monitor for DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) queries originating from BIG-IP management IP addresses — this is abnormal for network appliances and indicates BRICKSTORM C2 activity
Look for unusual HTTP 201 (Created) response codes paired with CSS content-type headers on BIG-IP management interfaces, which indicate C2 obfuscation technique
F5's built-in integrity checker detects modifications to system binaries; run periodically and alert on failures

what_to_look_for.description: Presence of this pipe file is a known BRICKSTORM indicator; it should not exist on uncompromised BIG-IP systems
BRICKSTORM modifies these system binaries for persistence; any hash change from baseline indicates compromise

what_to_look_for.indicator: DNS-over-HTTPS queries from BIG-IP management IP
what_to_look_for.description: Network appliances should not make DoH queries; this is a BRICKSTORM C2 communication pattern

what_to_look_for.indicator: HTTP 201 responses with CSS Content-Type from BIG-IP management interface
what_to_look_for.description: Attackers use HTTP 201 status codes with CSS content-type as a C2 obfuscation technique to blend into normal web traffic
Attackers scan and probe this REST API endpoint during reconnaissance; unusual access patterns indicate active exploitation attempts

what_to_look_for.indicator: apmd process crashes or unexpected restarts
what_to_look_for.description: Exploitation attempts against the apmd process may cause crashes before successful exploitation; monitor process restart frequency

immediate_actions.action: Patch F5 BIG-IP APM to fixed versions immediately
immediate_actions.description: Apply patches to all affected BIG-IP APM systems. Fixed versions: 17.1.0.4 or later for the 17.1.x branch, 16.1.4.3 or later for the 16.1.x branch, 15.1.10.2 or later for the 15.1.x branch. CISA deadline for federal agencies is March 30, 2026.
immediate_actions.command: tmsh show sys version && tmsh install sys software image <fixed-version>.iso

immediate_actions.action: Restrict management interface access if patching is delayed
immediate_actions.description: Immediately limit access to BIG-IP management interface (port 443/8443) and iControl REST API to trusted administrative IP ranges only using network ACLs or BIG-IP self IP port lockdown settings.
immediate_actions.command: tmsh modify sys global-settings mgmt-dhcp disabled && tmsh modify net self <self-ip> allow-service { tcp:443 }

immediate_actions.action: Run system integrity check on all BIG-IP APM appliances
immediate_actions.description: Execute F5's built-in integrity verification tool to detect BRICKSTORM modifications. Look for changes to /usr/bin/umount, /usr/sbin/httpd, unexpected files in /etc/sysconfig/, and presence of /run/bigtlog.pipe.

Review audit logs for suspicious iControl REST API calls from localhost, check for unusual outbound DNS-over-HTTPS connections from BIG-IP management IPs, and scan for HTTP 201 responses with CSS content-type headers in proxy/WAF logs.

immediate_actions.action: Engage F5 support if compromise is suspected
immediate_actions.description: If integrity check failures or IOC matches are found, immediately contact F5 Security Incident Response (security@f5.com), isolate the affected appliance from the network, preserve forensic evidence, and initiate incident response procedures.
